1. Introduction
---------------

More than 75% of emerging and re-emerging infectious diseases are zoonoses, which pose a heavy burden on human health and economies around the world ([Zhang et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib92)). Although existing molecular diagnostics and serological tests are capable of detecting zoonotic outbreaks, these methods are established to specific known pathogens, so emerging zoonoses may not be detected. In addition, serologic methods may not distinguish between current active infections and previous exposures, which will complicate threat assessment. Given these limitations, there is an urgent need to integrate advanced technologies like multi-omics approaches and remote sensing to complement traditional methods, which improves our ability to detect and respond to zoonotic diseases, particularly in early warning scenarios and spillover events.

Many studies have identified climatic factors as important driving forces for current zoonoses burden ([Rahman et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib64); [Zhang et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib93)), such as Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome ([Liang et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib49)), tick-borne encephalitis ([Tokarevich et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib74)), Lyme disease ([Porretta et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib62)), dengue fever ([Hales et al., 2002](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib35)), campylobacteriosis ([McBride et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib55)), etc. These observations stem from several underlying reasons. Firstly, climatic shifts have compelled pathogens and vectors to develop adaptive mechanisms that enhance the transmission of infections ([Irene et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib40)). Secondly, climate-induced alterations in ecosystems and habitats have led to changes in the distribution and migration patterns of wildlife. Consequently, this has brought animals and humans into closer proximity, thereby increasing the potential for zoonotic spillover ([Bartlow et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib6); [Caminade et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib18); [Magouras et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib53)).

In the coming decades, the global average temperature is expected to increase by 1.5–2°C, leading to more frequent and intense extreme weather events ([IPCC, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib39)). Hence, the need for early warning technologies and systems to enhance global preparedness against emerging zoonoses is further emphasized ([McMichael, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib56); [Leal Filho et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib48); [Rupasinghe et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib67)). As reviewed, [Worsley-Tonks et al. (2022)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib80) and [Tatiana Proboste et al. (2022)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib63) have proposed some innovative approaches to control environmental drivers and improve surveillance efficiency in resource-limited settings. Technologies such as remote sensing, environment-based screening, multi-omics analysis, and Internet surveillance could facilitate comprehensive and active monitoring of zoonotic species. However, to our knowledge, there is no literature yet that provides a feasible framework from an interdisciplinary systematic perspective. To respond, we first summarized and discussed various approaches for earlier surveillance. Based on existing multi-dimensional studies, we presented a decentralized zoonoses surveillance network under climate change impact ([Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#fig1)), which indicates that an interdisciplinary systematic framework is essential for early warning of zoonoses. Then we also discussed and proposed how to transform this network into a feasible and systematic framework for efficient alerting. The Susceptible-Exposed-Infectious-Recovered (SEIR) model, which is widely used in epidemiology to study and predict infectious diseases dynamics, was calibrated to support the early warning of zoonosis in animal populations. To our best knowledge, this is the first interdisciplinary systematic review of the application of environmental science, ecoscience, and big data on the advancing early warning and surveillance for zoonoses under climate change. It aims to integrate insights and findings from diverse scientific fields to enhance our understanding and preparedness for the emergence and spread of zoonoses in a changing climate.

2. Approaches for earlier surveillance
--------------------------------------

Studies on vectors and pathogens have indicated that the dynamics and interactions among humans, animals, and the environment are leading to blurred boundaries, which further increase zoonoses risks ([Wheeler and von Braun, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib78); [Abraham et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib1); [Gulia-Nuss et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib34); [Rulli et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib66); [Magouras et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib53); [Sun et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib72); [Sipari et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib70)). Remote sensing of flora and fauna ([He et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib38); [Wimberly et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib79)), analysis of environmental samples ([Amarasiri et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib4)), utilization of multi-omics techniques for disease mechanism investigations ([Zhu et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib94)), and internet-based methods for disease predictions ([Zhang et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib91)) are powerful strategies for early surveillance and control of pathogen transmission before outbreaks. These techniques are mainly aimed at shifting the focus from pathogens in clinical samples to potential pathogens in the environment, enabling early predictions ([Zinsstag et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib95); [Amarasiri et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib4); [Yang et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib86)).

### 2.1. Remote sensing surveillance

Climate change may lead to the spread of pathogens and vectors through host migration such as migratory birds ([Buczek et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib16)) and vectors ([Voyiatzaki et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib76)). The expansion of the geographic range of intermediate hosts and vector species may be an early sign of zoonoses ([Caminade et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib18)). Therefore, remote-based surveillance tools with the development of satellite remote sensing and statistical models are expected to enable global vector surveillance and pathogen spread monitoring ([Anenberg et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib5)). For instance, an outbreak risk mapping system has been developed by the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) and the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) in the United States for the surveillance of Rift Valley fever (RVF), which is caused by the RVF virus that can be transmitted to humans by mosquitoes ([Linthicum et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib51)). Based on the analysis of satellite vegetation indices, this RVF mapping system captured the monthly RVF risk in Africa and the Middle East, where associated with mosquito habitat ([Linthicum et al., 1999](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib50)). The model based on Pacific and Indian Ocean surface temperature anomalies and normalized difference vegetation index allows the prediction of regional RVF virus activity in Kenya 2–5 mon prior to the outbreak, thereby enabling effective activities such as vaccinating poultry or preparing pesticides to be performed in advance to prevent the spread of RVF.

In addition, the remote sensing data of plant diversity in combination with GPS track analysis of individual animals ([Kuenzer et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib44)) can be used for monitoring wildlife and analyzing migration patterns. For example, [Kranstauber et al. (2011)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib43) created a data model based on animal track data to describe animal movements in response to climate and land-use changes. This model can facilitate the comparison of data across different animal species and ecological niches, aiming to uncover potential relationships among climate change, animal behavior, and disease. [Orusa et al. (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib60) proposed a preliminary approach for continuous monitoring of alpine wildlife using Earth observation data, phenological indicators, and disease distribution. Given the role of animals in the spread of zoonoses, wildlife migration data and model analysis can be used for disease prediction.

Remarkably, the combination of statistical models with satellite remote sensing data has enabled better analysis of environmental and climate data. For example, [Benali et al. (2014)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib9) used not only the remote sensing data of air temperature and vegetation cover index but also statistical models to explore the relationship between vector density and environmental adaptability. This allowed them to compare current environmental conditions in Portugal with those of past vector epidemics and predict the potential impact of environmental change on malaria outbreak risk. In addition, [Campbell et al. (2022)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib19) used a spatiotemporal disease mapping model combined with the key Earth observation data to assess the impact of the environment on the transmission of arboviruses to chickens. However, the major limitation of current studies is the lack of long-term validation, which is often influenced by unpredictable factors. This suggests that more comprehensive satellite measurement data and in-depth exploration of correlations among climate change, animal behavior, and disease burden are desirable to create a dynamic map of areas at potentially high risk of zoonoses transmission.

Based on previous studies, we summarized a framework of remote sensing-based surveillance ([Fig.2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#fig2)). This framework is a seven-stage program that incorporates remote sensing technology into the development of disease early warning models. The first step involves determining the object and designing the sensing platform associated with the model subject based on data sources. The sensors collect spectroscopic and geometric information, which are processed through classification and calibration to build a remote sensing model. Once the remote sensing model is optimized, disease-related data can be incorporated to build a mathematical model. The primary objective of this framework is to facilitate researchers in the application of remote sensing technology with zoonoses surveillance, thereby enabling them to identify potential avenues for enhancement and innovation in this field.

### 2.2. Environmental surveillance

Environmental surveillance, including the analysis of samples collected from animal habitats, breeding facilities, livestock markets, etc., can provide useful information on the occurrence and distribution of pathogens, viruses, parasites, and other disease-causing agents ([Afema and Sischo, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib2); [Yanagihara et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib83)). To better represent the process of environmental surveillance, we have modified the flowchart to include upstream sampling and downstream analysis in [Fig.3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#fig3).

A modified flowchart of environment-based surveillance (Chemical and biological markers detected in the environment (e.g., animal habitats, health service departments, livestock sector, wastewater treatment plants, etc.) by Polymerase Chain Reaction ([Bohmann et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib12)), next-generation sequencing ([Cantalupo et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib20)), or rapid detection ([Yang et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib85)) provide a snapshot of human and animal health status).

The detection and analysis of various biomarkers, such as chemicals and pathogens in the environment is a powerful and cost-effective tool that provides important information to assess fingerprints of zoonoses transmission ([Yang et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib84); [Janse et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib41); [Mao et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib54); [Sims and Kasprzyk-Hordern, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib69)). For example, [Bohmann et al. (2018)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib12) used environmental DNA (eDNA) to determine the feeding patterns and population structure of vampire bats, which enabled the prediction of the vampire bat species at risk of spillover infection from bat-borne pathogens. [Casanovas-Massana et al. (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib22). Determined the molecular epidemiology and infection pathway of pathogenic _Leptospira_ by analyzing the genetic diversity of pathogenic _Leptospira_ in environmental samples such as surface water and soil. They also provided genetic evidence for the environmental spread of _leptospirosis_. Similarly, researchers analyzed water samples from _leptospirosis_ endemic areas in northern Okinawa, Japan, and found that 10 vertebrate species and 12 bacterial species were correlated with eDNA detection of _leptospirosis_ ([Sato et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib68)). _Leptospira_ species, _Leptospira_-related bacteria, and potential host vertebrates were summarized ([Sato et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib68)). [Cantalupo et al. (2011)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib20) identified 17 viruses that can infect humans by deep sequencing of nucleic acids from virions concentrated in untreated wastewater worldwide. The 234 viruses identified and a large number of unidentified viruses indicate that a large number of uncharacterized viruses exist in nature. Overall, the examples presented in the studies demonstrate the value of environmental sampling and analysis in exploring pathogen transmission pathways and identifying sources of infection.

Moreover, an increasingly wide range of portable biosensors for on-site detection of zoonotic bacteria has been reported in the recent literature. For instance, nanomaterials-based colorimetric methodologies functionalized with biomolecular receptors such as aptamers and antibodies have been developed for the detection of _Escherichia coli_ (a well-known Gram-negative bacterium that is related to several diseases and syndromes in humans and farm animals) ([Das et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib28); [Le et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib47))_,_ pathogenic _Salmonella_ strains (a food-borne bacterium that is involved in infectious Salmonellosis diseases in animals) ([Park et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib61); [Sun et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib71)) and _Brucella abortus_ (related to arguably the most considerable bacterial zoonoses Brucellosis of humans and animals) ([Taheri et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib73)). Other examples include aptamer-based fluorescent sensors for the detection of _Salmonella_ ([Wang et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib77)) and antibody-based electrochemical sensors for the detection of _Brucella melitensis_ ([Wu et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib81)), _Escherichia coli_ ([Muhammad-Tahir and Alocilja, 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib59)), and _Salmonella_ ([Zelada-Guillén et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib90)). These and similar on-site rapid surveillance technologies, when coupled with connectivity to the internet, can facilitate the collection, distribution, and analysis of real-time environmental pathogenic data. Some of the low-cost sensors can even be deployed via the public or non-governmental organizations to enable open-science projects in zoonoses. In addition to the detection methodologies which involve biomolecules such as DNA, nucleic acid, aptamers, and peptides, spectroscopic sensors without the need of biomolecular receptors for the detection of biomarkers (e.g., pharmaceuticals, antibiotics, benzodiazepines, endogenous compounds, etc.) have also been widely reported as a powerful technique for non-invasive detection. For example, cucurbiturial-based nanoaggregates-based surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) has been proposed as a biosensor for WBE by taking advantage of its inherent ultra-high sensitivity as well as the capability of multiplexed sensing ([Chio et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S167492782300151X#bib25)). However, the integration of portable biosensors and environmental biomarker analysis offers potential in addressing limitations of 1) lack of analysis of spatial and temporal impact factors by performing measurements at different sites with different time intervals, 2) limited investigations regarding the potential impacts of climate change on the activity and dynamic process of pathogens, which could contribute to preventing and controlling zoonoses under global climate change, as well as 3) few in-depth analysis of the data, such as analysis of the relative abundance of different virus species.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Data collection in zoonotic disease surveillance often faces limitations like incomplete datasets, lack of standardization, and insufficient geographic coverage, which hinder accurate assessments of disease dynamics.
Findings from specific regions or pathogens may not be applicable globally, limiting the ability to extrapolate results to broader contexts and potentially overlooking critical zoonotic threats.

Surveillance systems can suffer from biases due to selective reporting, prioritization of certain diseases over others, and socio-political influences, impacting the overall understanding of zoonotic disease epidemiology.
